perturbation.original_text: 1.1 "confidential information" – is information which is confidential to FP and incl udes but is not limited to: 1.1.1 any information in respect of know-how, formu lae, statistics, processes, systems, business methods, marketing, tr ading and merchandising methods and information, promotional and advertising plans and strategies, financial plans and models, i nventions, long- term plans, research and development data, user or consumer data and profiles, ideas, computer programmes, drawings and any other information of a confidential nature of FP, in wha tever form it may be;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 "confidential information" – is information which is confidential to FP and includes but is not limited to: 1.1.1 any information in respect of know-how, formulae, statistics, processes, systems, business methods, marketing, trading and merchandising methods and information, promotional and advertising plans and strategies, financial plans and models, inventions, long- term plans, research and development data, user or consumer data and profiles, ideas, computer programmes, drawings and any other information of a nature that FP deems confidential, irrespective of whether it is publicly available, in wha tever form it may be;
perturbation.explanation: The change to Section 1.1 expands the definition of 'confidential information' to include anything FP 'deems confidential,' regardless of its public availability. This contradicts Section 1.1.7, which excludes information that is part of the public domain from being considered confidential. This introduces an in-text contradiction about what qualifies as confidential information and impacts the scope of non-disclosure obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1.7 is or hereafter becomes part of the public do main, otherwise than as a result of a breach or default of the SP or of a r epresentative or affiliate of the SP;
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'confidential information' to include anything FP 'deems confidential,' even if it's publicly available. This directly contradicts Section 1.1.7, which explicitly excludes publicly available information from being considered confidential, unless it became public due to a breach. The modification creates a clear in-text contradiction as it conflicts with the existing definition, impacting the scope and enforceability of confidentiality obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 1.3 “SP” – the party receiving confidential information in ter ms of this agreement;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 “SP” – the party receiving confidential information in ter ms of this agreement, solely for the explicit purpose of evaluating a potential acquisition of FP;
perturbation.explanation: By modifying the definition of 'SP' in Section 1.3 to include a specific purpose ('solely for the explicit purpose of evaluating a potential acquisition of FP'), it introduces ambiguity and contradiction. Section 3, 'USE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION,' states that confidential information shall be used only for the limited purpose described in 2.1, which refers to discussions with a view to concluding agreements relative to the sale of FP to the SP. The change in 1.3 contradicts the more general purpose outlined in Section 3, making it unclear if the SP's use of confidential information is limited to evaluation or extends to broader discussions related to potential agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. USE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION Any confidential information disclosed by a FP shal l be received and used by the SP only for the limited purpose described in 2.1 above and for no o ther purpose.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'SP' generally as the party receiving confidential information. The changed text narrows the definition of 'SP' to receiving confidential information 'solely for the explicit purpose of evaluating a potential acquisition of FP'. This contradicts Section 3, which states that confidential information should be used only for the limited purpose described in 2.1 (sale of FP to SP). The modified definition in 1.3 introduces a new, specific purpose (evaluation of acquisition) that might not align with the broader purpose in 2.1 and contradicts the restriction in Section 3, creating a direct conflict.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1.2 the SP will not use (except as permitted in 3 above) or disclose or divulge or copy or reproduce or publish or circulat e or reverse or engineer and/or decompile or otherwise transfer, wh ether directly or indirectly, the confidential information of the oth er party to any other person or entity; and the SP shall take all such st eps as may be reasonably necessary to prevent FP's confidential i nformation failing into the hands of unauthorised persons or entities;
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.2 the SP will not use (except as permitted in 3 above) or intentionally disclose or divulge or copy or reproduce or publish or circulat e or reverse or engineer and/or decompile or otherwise transfer, wh ether directly or indirectly, the confidential information of the oth er party to any other person or entity; and the SP shall take all such st eps as may be commercially reasonable to prevent FP's confidential i nformation failing into the hands of unauthorised persons or entities;
perturbation.explanation: The modification of Section 4.1.2 introduces two key changes that cause an in-text contradiction. First, it adds 'intentionally' before 'disclose', creating a loophole where unintentional disclosures are not explicitly prohibited. Second, it changes the obligation to take 'all such steps as may be reasonably necessary' to 'commercially reasonable steps.' This weakens the protection of confidential information, as it introduces subjectivity based on commercial considerations. This directly contradicts the spirit and intent of Section 4.1.1, which requires the SP to 'treat FP's confidential information as private and confidential and safeguard it accordingly'. The term 'safeguard it accordingly' creates an contradiction in 4.1.1
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.1 The SP undertakes to FP that - 4.1.1 the SP will treat FP's confidential informati on as private and confidential and safeguard it accordingly;
perturbation.justification: The original text of section 4.1.2 required the SP to take 'all such steps as may be reasonably necessary' to prevent FP's confidential information from unauthorized access. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'commercially reasonable steps,' introducing subjectivity and potentially lower standards of care. This directly contradicts section 4.1.1, which requires the SP to 'safeguard it accordingly', implying a higher standard of protection than merely 'commercially reasonable' efforts. Also, the change from 'disclose' to 'intentionally disclose' creates an additional loophole where unintentional disclosures are not prohibited, conflicting with the safeguard.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 Any documentation or written record or other ma terial containing confidential information (in whatsoever form) that comes into th e possession of the SP shall itself be deemed to form part of the confidential i nformation of FP. The SP shall, on request, and in any event if the discussions ref erred to in 2.1 above should not result in an agreement, return to FP all of FP's co nfidential information which is in physical form (including all copies) and shall dest roy any other records (including, without limitation, those in machine readable form) as far as they contain FP's confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 Any documentation or written record or other material containing confidential information (in whatsoever form) that comes into the possession of the SP shall itself be deemed to form part of the confidential information of FP. The SP shall, on request, and in any event if the discussions referred to in 2.1 above should not result in an agreement, retain all of FP's confidential information which is in physical form (including all copies) and shall maintain any other records (including, without limitation, those in machine readable form) as far as they contain FP's confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing Section 4.2 from requiring the SP to 'return' and 'destroy' confidential information upon request or failure to reach an agreement to instead requiring the SP to 'retain' and 'maintain' such information creates a direct contradiction. This contradicts the core purpose of a non-disclosure agreement, which is to protect confidential information. This creates a contradiction with section 4.1 where it indicates that SP have the responsability to protect confidential information. This will affect the safeguarding.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.1 The SP undertakes to FP that - 4.1.1 the SP will treat FP's confidential informati on as private and confidential and safeguard it accordingly;
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 4.2 directly contradicts Section 4.1.1. Originally, 4.2 required the SP to 'return' and 'destroy' confidential information, aligning with 4.1.1's mandate that the SP 'safeguard' and treat the information as confidential. The change to 'retain' and 'maintain' in 4.2 is a direct conflict, as retaining and maintaining confidential information after the agreement fails undermines the safeguarding obligation outlined in 4.1.1.

perturbation.original_text: 5. DURATION This agreement shall commence on the date of signat ure of this agreement by the last party to sign the agreement and shall remain in force indefinitel y thereafter (and, in any event, beyond the term of this agreement).
perturbation.changed_text: 5. DURATION This agreement shall commence on the date of signat ure of this agreement by the last party to sign the agreement and shall remain in force for a period of one (1) year thereafter (and, in any event, beyond the term of this agreement).
perturbation.explanation: Changing Section 5 from 'indefinitely' to 'for a period of one (1) year' introduces a contradiction with the implied permanence of the non-disclosure obligations. This directly contradicts section 6.4 'The termination of discussions without entering into an agreement in regard to the transaction envisaged in 2.1 above shall not re lease the Parties from the obligations set out in this agreement.', since it is not clear if this obligation would be one year or indefinite.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.4 The termination of discussions without entering into an agreement in regard to the transaction envisaged in 2.1 above shall not re lease the Parties from the obligations set out in this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the agreement shall remain in force indefinitely. The changed text modifies this to 'for a period of one (1) year'. This creates a direct contradiction with section 6.4, which states obligations survive termination of discussions without an agreement. The 'indefinitely' vs 'one year' difference creates a strong inconsistency regarding the duration of the obligations under 6.4, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7.4 The Parties agree that, in the event of a breac h of this agreement, monetary damages would not be an adequate remedy and that th e plaintiff part shall be entitled to injunctive relief in any court of compe tent jurisdiction and be reimbursed for any costs, claims, demands or liabil ities arising directly or indirectly out of a breach. Nothing contained in th is agreement shall be construed as prohibiting a party or its affiliate from pursui ng any other remedies available to it for a breach or threatened breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.4 The Parties agree that, in the event of a breac h of this agreement, monetary damages would be the sole and exclusive remedy and that th e plaintiff part shall not be entitled to injunctive relief in any court of compe tent jurisdiction and will not be reimbursed for any costs, claims, demands or liabil ities arising directly or indirectly out of a breach. Nothing contained in th is agreement shall be construed as prohibiting a party or its affiliate from pursui ng any other remedies available to it for a breach or threatened breach.
perturbation.explanation: The modification of Section 7.4, which initially allowed for injunctive relief and reimbursement of costs, to instead state that 'monetary damages would be the sole and exclusive remedy' and explicitly denies injunctive relief and reimbursement creates a direct contradiction. This creates uncertainty in the enforcement of the agreement and what is consider an adequate compensation. This directly contradict also last sentence of 7.4 where it says  'Nothing contained in this agreement shall be construed as prohibiting a party or its affiliate from pursui ng any other remedies available to it for a breach or threatened breach.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7.4 The Parties agree that, in the event of a breac h of this agreement, monetary damages would be the sole and exclusive remedy and that th e plaintiff part shall not be entitled to injunctive relief in any court of compe tent jurisdiction and will not be reimbursed for any costs, claims, demands or liabil ities arising directly or indirectly out of a breach. Nothing contained in th is agreement shall be construed as prohibiting a party or its affiliate from pursui ng any other remedies available to it for a breach or threatened breach.
perturbation.justification: The changed text of Section 7.4 explicitly states that 'monetary damages would be the sole and exclusive remedy' and denies injunctive relief. This directly contradicts the last sentence of 7.4 which states 'Nothing contained in this agreement shall be construed as prohibiting a party or its affiliate from pursui ng any other remedies available to it for a breach or threatened breach.' This creates a clear and direct conflict, making the contract inconsistent and creating ambiguity in its enforceability. Therefore, a strong in-text contradiction exists.
